channel_name: Real Vision · Raoul Pal - The journey man
video_title: The Rise Of The New Wall Street | Raoul Pal & Tarek Mansour
gist: Kalshi founder Tarek Mansour tells Raoul Pal that regulated prediction markets and newly onshored perpetual futures are building a 'new Wall Street' that reverses financial history by handing pricing power back to a crowd of amateur super forecasters. He argues the company's forward-looking, falsifiable dataset is a unique AI-training asset, that most incumbents fight this via regulatory capture, and that anything open-ended will migrate from OTC/futures to on-exchange perpetuals.

themes.theme: New Wall Street & inverted market structure

insights.claim: Kalshi is explicitly positioning itself as a 'new Wall Street' by broadening the universe of tradable instruments so ordinary participants, not just incumbents, have places to win.
insights.speaker: Tarek Mansour (guest, Kalshi CEO)
insights.so_what: Signals Kalshi's ambition extends far beyond event betting into a broad exchange franchise; watch product breadth as the growth vector.
insights.quote: they called it like this idea of the rise of the new Wall Street like we're building a new Wall Street

insights.claim: Mansour argues most financial markets historically began as retail markets (early equities, FX, grain futures traded by farmers) and were later institutionalized by regulation that added gatekeeper barriers and intermediary chains.
insights.speaker: Tarek Mansour (guest, Kalshi CEO)
insights.so_what: Frames prediction markets as a re-democratization trend, implying long-run margin compression for gatekeeping intermediaries.

insights.claim: Regulation adds a 'gatekeeper premium' by wrapping products like credit default swaps in complexity that requires a chain of five intermediaries each taking a cut of the transaction.
insights.speaker: Raoul Pal (host)
insights.so_what: Identifies the fee stack that direct, simplified prediction/perpetual products are designed to disintermediate.

insights.claim: Most trades are a second derivative of the view a trader actually holds, forcing indirect expression and basis risk; prediction markets let you trade the underlying factor (inflation, an election, an earnings beat) directly.
insights.speaker: Tarek Mansour (guest, Kalshi CEO)
insights.so_what: Core product pitch: cleaner, lower-basis-risk expression of macro/political views than S&P options or single-stock proxies.
insights.quote: because almost all bets are second derivative of the actual bet you're trying to take

insights.claim: Mansour invokes Kevin Hassett's 'infinite markets' concept: society needs to price dimensions like AI, politics and geopolitics independently via prediction markets and feed them back into traditional asset prices, or pricing entropy grows over time.
insights.speaker: Tarek Mansour (guest, Kalshi CEO)
insights.so_what: Positions prediction-market data as an input into forecasting S&P, home prices and jobs, hinting at a data-licensing use case for macro allocators.

insights.claim: Mansour grounds prediction markets in Hayek's 1945 'knowledge problem': relevant information is distributed, dynamic and localized, and opening a free market on a question is the proven most effective way to surface it.
insights.speaker: Tarek Mansour (guest, Kalshi CEO)
insights.so_what: Intellectual moat framing; markets as information-aggregation beats surveys and expert panels.

insights.claim: Mansour cites the 'Superforecasters' finding that self-selected, educated non-experts, aggregated, outperform domain experts once given time to analyze, calling it a counterintuitive result that domain expertise is not an important dimension for prediction.
insights.speaker: Tarek Mansour (guest, Kalshi CEO)
insights.so_what: Undercuts the value of institutional research edge; the crowd, not the credentialed analyst, prices the tail.
insights.quote: it's a pretty counterintuitive finding that domain expertise is not an important dimension

insights.claim: Money is the incentive mechanism that raises the quality of Kalshi's forecaster crowd, distinguishing it from the ~15 Bloomberg economists whose consensus carries shared professional biases.
insights.speaker: Tarek Mansour (guest, Kalshi CEO)
insights.so_what: Skin-in-the-game aggregation is pitched as structurally more accurate than sell-side economist surveys for data prints.

insights.claim: Prediction markets invert social-media incentives: the boring, down-the-middle take makes money while extreme, passionate positions generally lose money.
insights.speaker: Tarek Mansour (guest, Kalshi CEO)
insights.so_what: Positions Kalshi as a de-polarizing 'truth' signal versus clickbait feeds; a differentiator for institutional trust in the data.
insights.quote: That boring take in the middle makes money.

insights.claim: Kalshi's winning traders are a small elite of 'intellectual athletes' with no shared demographic pattern across gender, age, income or Ivy League status, echoing the superforecaster research.
insights.speaker: Tarek Mansour (guest, Kalshi CEO)
insights.so_what: Implies edge is a scarce, trainable skill rather than an institutional resource, supporting the 'back the superstar' marketplace idea.
insights.quote: some random cur curiosity gene that they have that allows them to do this

insights.claim: Kalshi's best inflation forecaster is described as 'a random dude in Kansas' who simply reads company news, not a systematized or AI-driven operation.
insights.speaker: Tarek Mansour (guest, Kalshi CEO)
insights.so_what: Concrete proof point that non-institutional participants can out-forecast Wall Street on macro data.
insights.quote: our best inflation forecaster is a random dude in Kansas

insights.claim: The most effective Kalshi traders trade value rather than direction, buying below and selling above their estimated fair value (e.g. buy below 62%, sell above 67% on a 65% fair value), and this continuous mean-reversion is what produces the market's calibration.
insights.speaker: Tarek Mansour (guest, Kalshi CEO)
insights.so_what: Describes the market-microstructure mechanism behind Kalshi's accuracy; edge comes from fair-value estimation, not momentum.
insights.quote: I will buy it for anything on 62% and below and I will sell it for anything at 67% and above

insights.claim: Trader 'Domer' profited by buying the American pope at 1% odds on Kalshi purely because 1% was mispriced, illustrating that humans fail to intuit the large difference between a 1% and a 10% probability.
insights.speaker: Tarek Mansour (guest, Kalshi CEO)
insights.so_what: Low-probability mispricing is a repeatable edge; small percentages carry dramatically different risk and are systematically undervalued.
insights.quote: he didn't think he was going to win. He just thought 1% is wrong

insights.claim: Mansour argues COVID was mispriced by markets as a 1% event when it was clearly a 10-20% event that should have been alarming by December 2019, a fat-tail miscalibration.
insights.speaker: Tarek Mansour (guest, Kalshi CEO)
insights.so_what: Reinforces that better low-probability calibration is where prediction markets add risk-management value.
insights.quote: it was not a 1% event that was clearly a 10 to 20% event which should have been incredibly alarming back in December of 2019

insights.claim: Perpetual futures are the fastest-growing product Kalshi has ever launched, and its perps use the same traditional CME-style risk methodology with no more leverage than incumbent futures.
insights.speaker: Tarek Mansour (guest, Kalshi CEO)
insights.so_what: Regulated, non-exotic leverage framing is the wedge for mainstreaming perps in the US; watch adoption vs offshore venues.

insights.claim: Perpetuals are a fundamentally better product than rolling futures because a six-month view no longer requires buying monthly futures and paying rollover fees six times, and they let retail short symmetrically without the difficulty of shorting spot.
insights.speaker: Tarek Mansour (guest, Kalshi CEO)
insights.so_what: Rollover-fee elimination threatens roughly 20% of incumbent futures revenue and lowers the barrier to retail shorting.
insights.quote: you have to buy the monthly future and you have to roll over so you pay fees six times

insights.claim: Bitcoin perpetuals are gaining fast traction because they match how people actually hold a view (bullish 'for now,' exit when the thesis materializes) without needing to reason about dividends, cash flows or a fixed expiry.
insights.speaker: Tarek Mansour (guest, Kalshi CEO)
insights.so_what: Explains the product-market fit driving Kalshi's crypto growth; expect expansion to more digital-asset perps first.

insights.claim: Perpetuals date back to Robert Shiller's 1990s proposal for underlyings with no natural expiry, and the crypto industry (Hyperliquid and others) speedran the concept offshore before Kalshi brought it onshore in a regulated form.
insights.speaker: Tarek Mansour (guest, Kalshi CEO)
insights.so_what: Kalshi's repeatable playbook: take a proven-offshore or theoretical instrument and mainstream it via US regulation.

insights.claim: Mansour predicts anything open-ended, whether equities, FX or some types of energy, will inevitably migrate to a perpetual structure.
insights.speaker: Tarek Mansour (guest, Kalshi CEO)
insights.so_what: A sweeping bull case for perps cannibalizing dated futures across asset classes; long-run structural threat to expiry-based exchanges.

insights.claim: The right use of leverage is to 'normalize volatility': apply baseline leverage to low-volatility products so they trade actively, and restrain leverage on high-volatility products, making 200x leverage an inappropriate extreme.
insights.speaker: Tarek Mansour (guest, Kalshi CEO)
insights.so_what: A risk-design principle differentiating Kalshi from 200x offshore venues and courting institutional comfort.
insights.quote: normalize volatility is the exact term

insights.claim: Humans structurally crave leverage, which Mansour and Pal frame as central to perpetuals' appeal, with Pal noting people love leverage more than almost anything.
insights.speaker: Raoul Pal (host)
insights.so_what: Behavioral driver behind perp volumes; also the risk vector regulation is meant to fence.
insights.quote: They love sex and leverage more than anything else.

insights.claim: Kalshi treats agricultural perpetuals cautiously because farmers and the ag community are not ready for 24/7 weekend volatility on products tied to food security.
insights.speaker: Tarek Mansour (guest, Kalshi CEO)
insights.so_what: Signals which markets Kalshi will and won't push into perps first; ag is a deliberate hold-back.

insights.claim: Kalshi's calibration plots, including ones referenced from the Federal Reserve, are near-perfect: events priced at 80% happen about 80% of the time.
insights.speaker: Tarek Mansour (guest, Kalshi CEO)
insights.so_what: Empirical validation that the price is an accurate probability, strengthening the case to license the data as ground truth.
insights.quote: when we say something has 80% chance of happening, it basically ends up happening 80% of the time

insights.claim: Prediction markets are least accurate around the 50% level because a 50/50 price often reflects a high-entropy, no-information event rather than a genuine coin flip, a bias Kevin Hassett proved mathematically and Kalshi observes in practice.
insights.speaker: Tarek Mansour (guest, Kalshi CEO)
insights.so_what: Tells traders and data users that mid-book prices carry the least information; edge concentrates away from 50%.
insights.quote: when we say something is 50/50 we're the least accurate

insights.claim: Kalshi observes a persistent 'yes' bias where participants prefer buying yes over no, creating natural buy-side flow.
insights.speaker: Tarek Mansour (guest, Kalshi CEO)
insights.so_what: A structural mispricing that premium-sellers can systematically fade for edge.
insights.quote: People just like saying yes more than no.

insights.claim: Prediction markets currently price on current-day news flow and reassessed odds rather than a slower-moving long end of the curve, leaving longer-dated probability pricing underdeveloped and, per Pal, highly valuable.
insights.speaker: Raoul Pal (host)
insights.so_what: Identifies the long-dated/term-structure opportunity as the next frontier where the real forecasting money is made.

insights.claim: Perpetuals add an unknown time horizon on top of an unknown outcome, a new skill set that suits intuitive macro thinkers but will challenge many participants at first.
insights.speaker: Raoul Pal (host)
insights.so_what: Adoption friction to watch; perps may favor macro-native traders early on.

insights.claim: Financial exchanges have uniquely reflexive liquidity: unlike Airbnb where more listings don't drive more transactions, adding liquidity to a market makes participants transact more, producing second- and third-order network effects once the flywheel starts.
insights.speaker: Tarek Mansour (guest, Kalshi CEO)
insights.so_what: Explains why the cold-start is hard but the incumbent advantage compounds; supports Kalshi's >90% share durability.

insights.claim: Kalshi holds over 90% market share and expects it to erode somewhat over time, but argues liquidity begets liquidity so the market is far from saturation and welcomes competition.
insights.speaker: Tarek Mansour (guest, Kalshi CEO)
insights.so_what: Quantifies dominant incumbency; the liquidity network effect is the key competitive moat to monitor.
insights.quote: we're over 90% market share now

insights.claim: Exchanges make more money selling data than from transactions, and Kalshi is currently giving its data away free and open-source to maximize adoption before eventually monetizing it.
insights.speaker: Tarek Mansour (guest, Kalshi CEO)
insights.so_what: Flags a future revenue line; the free-now stance is a land-grab, and monetization is an eventual re-rate catalyst.

insights.claim: Kalshi's dataset is uniquely forward-looking and falsifiable (each prediction resolves, so a model can learn what predicting well takes) across a diversity no one else has, which Mansour concedes could support building a frontier model.
insights.speaker: Tarek Mansour (guest, Kalshi CEO)
insights.so_what: Reframes Kalshi as an AI-data company; the falsification structure is what makes the data valuable for training predictive models.

insights.claim: Mansour hints Kalshi is actively considering monetizing or restricting its data rather than continuing to hand it to AI labs for free.
insights.speaker: Tarek Mansour (guest, Kalshi CEO)
insights.so_what: A telegraphed strategic pivot; a shift from free-to-labs to paid data would be a material business change.
insights.quote: people should not be surprised if you end up doing something on that front

insights.claim: Pal links Kalshi to Ribbit Capital's 'token factory' thesis (from Micky Malka's team), arguing the move from AGI to ASI needs humanity-scale data and Kalshi is effectively a token factory of global probabilities that agents will pay to price.
insights.speaker: Raoul Pal (host)
insights.so_what: Positions the data marketplace as potentially larger than today's financial markets given civilizational-scale AI demand.
insights.quote: you are a gigantic token factories of global probabilities of all sorts of things

insights.claim: In a world of abundant data, synthesis has become the core bottleneck to surfacing truth, and markets are a compression mechanism that collapses all available information to a single number between 0 and 100.
insights.speaker: Tarek Mansour (guest, Kalshi CEO)
insights.so_what: Positions market prices as the ultimate data-compression/synthesis layer, a durable value prop as information overload worsens.
insights.quote: synthesis is becoming incredibly difficult like that that's actually the the core bottleneck

insights.claim: More than 80% of Kalshi's active users do not trade and instead use it as a newsfeed and source of truth to check probabilities of events.
insights.speaker: Tarek Mansour (guest, Kalshi CEO)
insights.so_what: Reveals a large non-trading media/data audience, supporting a distinct data or advertising monetization path beyond transaction fees.

insights.claim: Mansour claims Kalshi is the fastest-growing company outside of Anthropic right now, attributing the surge to people seeking better ways to filter an overload of information.
insights.speaker: Tarek Mansour (guest, Kalshi CEO)
insights.so_what: A headline growth claim benchmarking Kalshi against the leading AI lab; a strong (if self-reported) momentum signal.

insights.claim: Kalshi spent about four years and had to sue the US government to win regulated status for prediction markets, ultimately being vindicated after most people said it would never be allowed.
insights.speaker: Tarek Mansour (guest, Kalshi CEO)
insights.so_what: The regulatory moat was won at great cost, a barrier competitors must also clear; perseverance is the competitive edge.
insights.quote: We had to sue the government.

insights.claim: Mansour argues much financial regulation is fundamentally business-interest-driven incumbent protection built through decades of lobbying and regulatory capture, not just consumer protection.
insights.speaker: Tarek Mansour (guest, Kalshi CEO)
insights.so_what: Explains why incumbents fight via rulemaking; regulatory battles, not product, are the main competitive front.
insights.quote: it's fundamentally uh business interest driven

insights.claim: The CFTC has been sued over its approval of perpetuals, which Mansour reads as a validating signal because incumbents litigating rather than embracing a product indicates they are scared of it.
insights.speaker: Tarek Mansour (guest, Kalshi CEO)
insights.so_what: Frames incumbent lawsuits as a contrarian buy-signal on the disruptor; watch the litigation outcome as a catalyst.
insights.quote: when an incumbent is suing over a product instead of like um embracing it, that's kind of a good sign for the innovators

insights.claim: Mansour's regulatory philosophy is to 'build a highway wide enough for competition and innovators to thrive' but with a fence so consumers can't fall off the cliff, rejecting narrow choke-point regulation.
insights.speaker: Tarek Mansour (guest, Kalshi CEO)
insights.so_what: A pro-regulation posture that courts institutional and regulator trust as a competitive weapon against unregulated rivals.
insights.quote: build a highway that is wide enough for competition and innovators to thrive and do the things they want to do

insights.claim: Some incumbents break the innovator's dilemma by investing in or building competing platforms, while others choose to litigate to squash innovation, which Mansour says is rarely the winning side of history in America.
insights.speaker: Tarek Mansour (guest, Kalshi CEO)
insights.so_what: A lens for handicapping which legacy exchanges adapt versus decline as perps and prediction markets scale.

insights.claim: Global regulation is a fragmented mess, so Kalshi's international expansion will require going regulator-by-regulator the hard, regulated-first way, which is itself the barrier to entry that protects the opportunity.
insights.speaker: Tarek Mansour (guest, Kalshi CEO)
insights.so_what: International rollout will be slow and jurisdiction-heavy; the friction is both the risk and the moat.

themes.theme: New asset classes & democratized asset management

insights.claim: Kalshi launched a 'Kalshi American Power Index' that aggregates prediction-market data across every seat and margin of victory in the Senate, House, Supreme Court and presidency into a single index oscillating between +50 Republican and +50 Democrat, pitched as 'the S&P for politics.'
insights.speaker: Tarek Mansour (guest, Kalshi CEO)
insights.so_what: A productized political-risk index; backtested to tilt Democrat at the Iran-war peak and back Republican after Virginia redistricting and ceasefire talks.

insights.claim: Mansour sees a large biotech/FDA-approval market opportunity because roughly 95% of drug companies fail yet get funded for years by charismatic CEOs, and a market pricing approval odds would cut sales leverage and give patients an independent data point.
insights.speaker: Tarek Mansour (guest, Kalshi CEO)
insights.so_what: A concrete new-market roadmap item with capital-allocation implications for biotech funding and clinical-trial transparency.
insights.quote: 95% of these companies fail and they get funded for so long

insights.claim: Moving a risk from over-the-counter to on-exchange has historically expanded the market by a factor of 10 to 50x, which is the multiplier Kalshi is targeting across insurance-like risks.
insights.speaker: Tarek Mansour (guest, Kalshi CEO)
insights.so_what: Quantifies the TAM expansion thesis for on-exchange insurance, weather and event risk versus incumbent OTC pricing.
insights.quote: every time an over-the-c counter market has moved to on exchange rate, the market blew by like a factor of like 10 to 50x

insights.claim: Kalshi prices parametric hurricane and weather risk that pays out immediately when a hurricane hits a town, contrasting with traditional insurance that can take two years and requires proving damages.
insights.speaker: Tarek Mansour (guest, Kalshi CEO)
insights.so_what: Parametric, instant-payout products are an active wedge into the insurance market where few super forecasters currently price risk.
insights.quote: with traditional insurance, oftentimes it takes two years to get paid

insights.claim: Businesses like bars are using Kalshi to hedge economic exposure to game outcomes (buying 'sports insurance' on-exchange for cheaper prices than OTC) to smooth their P&L against different results.
insights.speaker: Tarek Mansour (guest, Kalshi CEO)
insights.so_what: Evidence of real-economy commercial hedging demand, broadening Kalshi's user base beyond speculators.

insights.claim: Mansour and Pal riff on Shiller-style perpetuals for real estate (long a city, short a city to hedge a home) and human capital (a perp on future earnings, e.g. a basket of MIT graduates shorted against Harvard, to pay off student loans in advance).
insights.speaker: Raoul Pal (host)
insights.so_what: Sketches speculative but structurally coherent new perpetual asset classes for hedging home and human-capital risk.
insights.quote: go long a city go short a city

insights.claim: Pal proposes tokenizing individual traders' performance so anyone can effectively become a hedge fund, collapsing the pod and mothership structure of a firm like Millennium and letting capital be allocated to 50 pods of real forecasters in real time.
insights.speaker: Raoul Pal (host)
insights.so_what: A vision for unbundling asset management; a marketplace of super forecasters raising external capital on track record alone.
insights.quote: It collapses all of the structure of the pods at Millennium.

insights.claim: Kalshi customers who have gone full-time are already raising external money on their track records, pointing toward a meritocratic marketplace for pricing capital that bypasses traditional hedge-fund gatekeeping.
insights.speaker: Tarek Mansour (guest, Kalshi CEO)
insights.so_what: Early signal that forecaster talent is being institutionalized into fundable managers, a new allocation channel.

insights.claim: Mansour predicts banks will embrace prediction markets faster than they embraced crypto, both to integrate the pricing data or be left behind and to trade positions like the JD Vance, Rubio, Gavin Newsom or AOC 2028 outcomes directly instead of via proxies.
insights.speaker: Tarek Mansour (guest, Kalshi CEO)
insights.so_what: Institutional adoption is the key growth catalyst; watch bank integration through the midterms and 2028 election cycle.
insights.quote: a lot of them are actually starting to embrace it faster than I think they embrace crypto
